critic_raw:
This multi-task deep learning vs. logistic regression experiment for patient readmission prediction has several methodological problems that could invalidate its conclusions.

1. RANDOM SPLIT ON LONGITUDINAL EHR DATA VIOLATES TEMPORAL CAUSALITY. The design uses 'stratified random sampling across all available months, holding out a random 15% of observations as the test set.' With 60 months of EHR data, this means a patient discharged in month 3 can appear in training while that same patient's subsequent admission in month 45 can appear in validation or test. The model is not being evaluated on its ability to predict future readmissions from past data — it is interpolating between observed events. This is not a deployment-realistic evaluation and will systematically overstate performance compared to a prospective deployment scenario.

2. AUXILIARY TASK LABEL (LENGTH OF STAY) IS COMPUTED FROM THE INDEX ADMISSION AND THUS AVAILABLE AT PREDICTION TIME. The LOS regression head uses 'length of stay for the index hospitalization' as the auxiliary target. LOS is computed during the admission — meaning it is not a future outcome but a current observation. Using an observed quantity as an auxiliary supervision signal is not multi-task learning of a clinically meaningful joint distribution; it is regularization with an identity-adjacent target. Any benefit attributed to 'multi-task learning' may simply reflect that LOS is a strong feature for readmission that the model is being trained to memorize rather than predict.

3. CLINICAL BERT EMBEDDINGS ARE NOT FIT ON TRAINING DATA ONLY BUT THE VOCABULARY AND TOKENIZATION ARE. The design states preprocessing fit exclusively on the training set, but Clinical BERT is a pre-trained model that was trained on general biomedical corpora — not on this hospital's discharge notes. If the clinical BERT tokenizer or vocabulary does not cover institution-specific abbreviations and note styles, it will silently drop or split tokens in a way that degrades representation quality. The design does not address this domain mismatch risk.

4. AUROC AS PRIMARY METRIC DOES NOT CAPTURE INTERVENTION THRESHOLDING. The stated operational goal is to 'minimize both false positives and false negatives.' AUROC measures ranking ability but says nothing about calibration or the operating point that will actually be used. A clinical intervention requires choosing a threshold. The design selects the operating threshold on validation to achieve ≥90% sensitivity — but this threshold is applied to both train-val models and the final model, introducing a second hyperparameter that was tuned on validation. This threshold selection is a form of post-hoc test-set optimization if the sensitivity target was chosen after seeing validation results.

5. THE MULTI-TASK BASELINE COMPARISON CONFOUNDS ARCHITECTURE WITH FEATURE SET. The multi-task model receives clinical BERT embeddings plus TF-IDF features. The logistic regression baseline receives 'demographics, Charlson index, admission type, diagnosis codes, and utilization features' — no text at all. If the multi-task model outperforms logistic regression, it is unclear whether this is due to (a) deep architecture, (b) temporal sequence modeling, (c) the clinical text features, or (d) the auxiliary LOS task. These cannot be disentangled without ablation conditions — at minimum a logistic regression trained on the full feature set including BERT embeddings.
all_issues_raised: Random stratified split across 60 months allows future admissions of same patient to appear in training while earlier admissions appear in test, violating temporal causality
Length-of-stay auxiliary target is an observed index-admission quantity, not a future outcome; using it as auxiliary supervision conflates regularization with multi-task learning
Clinical BERT trained on general biomedical corpora may silently degrade on institution-specific note styles without detection
Threshold selection at 90% sensitivity on validation data constitutes a tuned hyperparameter that inflates apparent test-set calibration

Architecture comparison confounds model class, feature set (text vs. no text), and auxiliary task simultaneously; no ablation distinguishes the contribution of clinical notes from multi-task structure
Stratified random split across 60 months allows training on same time period as test, violating temporal ordering
Discharge summaries encode clinician readmission prognosis, creating a label-leakage vector
Missing efficacy labels concentrated among highest-risk patients introduces selection bias in the auxiliary task
Imputing absent comorbidities as zero systematically underestimates risk in complex poorly-documented patients
MTL efficiency claim conflates implicit regularization with representation-sharing benefit
Stratified random split in 60-month temporal EHR data enables anachronistic training on future records
Multi-task auxiliary LOS objective provides extra training signal not available to the logistic regression baseline, confounding the comparison
AUROC as primary metric is insensitive to the threshold-dependent operational decision; clinically calibrated PPV/sensitivity is more relevant
Concatenating TF-IDF and clinical BERT embeddings introduces collinearity that may disproportionately degrade the logistic regression baseline
